region: Eastern Washington Alpine - Stuart Range, Leavenworth, Wenatchee Range
dataSource: Mountain Project, SummitPost, WTA, Alpine guides, trip reports

routes.name: Mount Stuart - Cascadian Couloir

trailheads.name: Esmeralda Trailhead (Recommended)
trailheads.access: From Cle Elum: Hwy 970 east 8.5 mi → Teanaway Rd 13 mi → FR 9737 9.6 mi

trailheads.name: Stuart Lake Trailhead
trailheads.access: From Leavenworth: Icicle Creek Rd 8.4 mi → FR 7601 3.7-4 mi

approach.terrain: Forested switchbacks, alpine talus, scree meadows, granite scrambling

routeBeta.description: Follow Cascadian Couloir on bearing 348° through gully systems. Ascend 2,000-3,000 ft of 40-45° loose rock, sand, and scree to Sherpa Col. Summit ridge traverse (Class 3-4) with significant exposure to true summit.
routeBeta.technicalDescription: 60-70% talus/scree scrambling (Class 2-3), 20-30% seasonal snow/ice (may require crampons), 10% exposed rock scrambling (Class 3-4)

crux.location: Upper Cascadian Couloir and summit pyramid
crux.description: 2,000-3,000 ft of steep, extremely loose rock, sand, and scree. Largest boulders held in place by seasonal freeze-thaw cycles. Final summit pyramid approach via Class 3-4 exposed granite ridge.

alternatives.name: West Ridge
alternatives.character: 3rd-4th class scrambling with 5.6 crux at summit

hazards.description: Extremely loose, unstable rock throughout. Helmets mandatory year-round. Massive freeze-thaw damage in upper couloir.
hazards.mitigation: Helmet essential, tight rope teams to minimize exposure

hazards.description: Several hundred feet of Class 3 scrambling with significant exposure. High fall consequence.
hazards.mitigation: Careful footwork, spot each other on exposed sections

hazards.description: Early season cornices, hard icy snow; steep sections prone to slides
Multiple gully options; easy to deviate onto harder terrain or loose sections
Cornices above Longs Pass and summit ridge

ascent.notes: Highly variable by conditions and experience

descent.notes: Snow descent much faster (glissading possible); scree slower
Snow descent much faster than scree. Glissading possible. Controlled descent recommended on loose rock.

alternatives.name: Headlight Basin
alternatives.notes: Late season ski/descent option, longer but sometimes safer in high-cornice years

alternatives.name: Sherpa Glacier
alternatives.notes: Early season only (May-June), steep (~40°) but reliable snow

seasons.name: Early Season
seasons.conditions: Cornices, hard icy snow (crampons mandatory), avalanche potential, roaring creeks, avalanche debris may block road

seasons.name: Mid-Summer
seasons.conditions: Loose scree/talus maximizes rockfall, warm temps, rapid creek swelling from melt

seasons.name: Late Season
seasons.conditions: Early/late day ice, unpredictable snow-ice transitions, still dangerous creek crossings

routes.notableFeature: Second highest non-volcanic mountain in Washington; largest single mass of exposed granite in North America per Fred Beckey

routes.name: Mount Stuart - North Ridge

trailheads.name: Ingalls Creek/Esmeralda Trailhead (Southern)
trailheads.access: From Cle Elum: Hwy 970 east 8.5 mi → Teanaway Rd 13 mi → FR 9737 9.6 mi

trailheads.name: Lake Stuart/Colchuck Lake (Northern)
trailheads.access: From Leavenworth: Icicle Creek Rd 9.2 mi → Bridge Creek Campground → FR 7601 3.5 mi

timeToBaseCamp.notes: 5-6 hours to glacier base via southern approach

approach.terrain: Trail hiking to lake, alpine scrambling, glacier crossing, rock climbing terrain

routeBeta.description: Classic alpine climbing on the north ridge crest of Mount Stuart. Fred Beckey: 'Single greatest mass of exposed granite in the United States'

routeVariations.name: Upper North Ridge
routeVariations.climbingGrade: 5.4 line of least resistance OR 5.7 ridge crest

routeVariations.name: Standard North Ridge
routeVariations.reference: Fifty Classic Climbs of North America

routeBeta.technicalComposition: Mix of 5.5-5.6 climbing with 4th class and low 5th class simulclimbing; belayed crux sections

directOption.pitch1: Steep fingers-and-hands dihedral with good resting points
directOption.pitch2: Technical short off-width requiring solid fist-jamming

bypassOption.description: 75-foot rappel off right side below buttress, traverse gully on snow/wet/icy rock (low 5th class), 4-5 additional pitches of class 4-5 to summit

hazards.description: Constant exposure on granite throughout ridge
Possible on upper sections and descent routes
Loose 3rd class terrain; 'one of the worst climbing experiences'; descending back across mountain
Early season only (June-mid-July); impassable after mid-summer
18-21 hour car-to-car day is extremely demanding

climbing: 60-meter half rope (twin configuration recommended) or doubled 60m
footwear: Technical climbing shoes for harder pitches
Crampons and ice axe (for glacier descent; essential for Sherpa Glacier)

timeEstimates.alternativeStyle: 3-day trip with overnight camps at Ingalls Lake or ridge base (5,400 ft)

descent.notes: Class 3-4 scrambling, shortest descent. MAJOR DRAWBACK: Loose rock; 'one of the worst climbing experiences'. Requires circling mountain if approaching from north.

alternatives.name: Sherpa Glacier
alternatives.notes: More straightforward than Couloir, full day

alternatives.name: Traverse variant
alternatives.notes: If bypassing Great Gendarme on ascent, alternate descent via class 4-5 terrain

routes.seasonalWindow: June-mid-July (Sherpa Glacier negotiable), late July-September (southern approach only)
routes.firstAscent: June 1956 (Don Gordon and John Rupley, via direct North Ridge bypass)
notableFacts: Listed in Fifty Classic Climbs of North America
Fred Beckey: 'Single greatest mass of exposed granite in the United States'
Described as 'one of the best routes in the range'

summary.dataCompleteness: Comprehensive - GPS coordinates, trailheads, approach details, route grades, hazards, gear requirements, time estimates, and descent descriptions documented for all routes
summary.nextSteps: Import structured data into Supabase routes table; cross-reference with areas hierarchical structure
